tasks.chapterTitle: Reductions, Content Words, and Teacher Resources

messages.content:
This textbook module, *Green Tea: Intermediate English Communication*, serves as a comprehensive resource for second-language learners, focusing on integrating social topics with essential language skills (p. 3).

### **Core Curriculum Overview**
The course is structured to develop communicative competence through the following key areas:

*   **Personality & Psychology:** Explores the characteristics of introverts, extroverts, and ambiverts, as well as the role of empathy in cross-cultural communication (p. 11, p. 37, p. 49).
*   **Sports & Accessibility:** Examines the history and impact of the Paralympic Games, fostering discussion on disability awareness, athlete motivation, and the criteria for inclusive sports (p. 17, p. 29, p. 41).
*   **Cultural & Social Phenomena:** Investigates the origins of superstitions and their influence on daily life and work settings (p. 43, p. 45, p. 47).
*   **Health & Wellness:** Includes units on brain health (specifically aphasia and stroke awareness) and practical lessons on nutrition and healthy lifestyle choices (p. 49, p. 59).
*   **News & Literacy:** Provides a series of "ESOL News" articles covering local topics, such as regional developments in Oregon and human-interest stories (p. 71–86).

### **Instructional Methodology**
The curriculum employs research-grounded methods to support language acquisition:
*   **Pronunciation Focus:** Systematic practice of vowel sounds, consonant clusters, word stress, and the phonological rules for regular past tense (-ed) and plural (-s) verb endings (p. 91–111).
*   **Active Learning:** Incorporates interactive lectures, note-taking exercises, and recording assignments to improve listening and speaking fluency (p. 13, p. 20, p. 22). 
*   **Vocabulary Development:** Uses thematic word lists and context-based exercises to build academic and conversational vocabulary (p. 5, p. 7).
*   **Analytical Thinking:** Encourages students to synthesize information through discussions, personal reflections (e.g., the "I AM" project), and group activities that require critical evaluation (p. 23, p. 31, p. 45).

Lesson 3: Intermediate English Communication (Green Tea) - Presentation9 slides
Intermediate English Communication
  • Focusing on high-intermediate communication skills.
  • Exploring personality, diversity, and language nuances.

This unit explores the psychological concepts of introversion and extroversion, focusing on how different personality types function and gain energy. The content is organized as follows:

Core Concepts
The material clarifies that introversion and extroversion are not "black and white" categories but rather a spectrum [S.16]. A major focus is correcting the misconception that introverts are simply shy; instead, the distinction lies in how individuals recharge their energy [S.16, S.20]. Introverts typically replenish energy through solitary reflection and contemplation of internal ideas, whereas extroverts interact differently [S.17, S.18, S.20]. The term "ambivert" is introduced to describe those who fall into the gray area between these two types [S.13, S.16].

Learning Objectives
The unit encourages students to define these terms, identify personal personality traits, and recognize the strengths and weaknesses associated with each type [S.13, S.19]. It emphasizes the importance of being considerate of the diverse ways people function within social settings [S.16].

Vocabulary and Pronunciation
To support language development, the text introduces key academic and social terminology, including words such as $unique$, $misconception$, $contemplating$, and $individuals$ [S.15, S.17]. Pronunciation exercises are provided to help learners master specific phonetic challenges, such as the $ch/j$ distinction, $th$ sounds, and past tense verb endings [S.21, S.23]. Key past tense verbs identified in the practice include: thought, heard, read, spoke, understood, knew, saw, were, and found [S.22, S.23].

This document focuses on the intersection of athletics, physical limitations, and aging, providing educational resources and activities for students to explore these topics. The key points are summarized as follows:

**Educational Presentations on Parasports**
The materials introduce students to the history and nature of the Paralympic Games. These sections include interactive presentations covering famous athletes, the history of the sport, and inquiries into specific impairments, adapted equipment, and practical advice for athletes.

**Athlete Profiles and Personal Reflection**
Students are encouraged to analyze the life and motivations of Olympic and lifelong athletes, such as Michael Hubbs and Mr. Dharam Singh. The exercises ask students to reflect on their own lives by identifying personal challenges, accomplishments, and motivational drivers. This includes writing an "I AM" statement as a way to define one’s identity, passions, and goals.

**Language and Communication Skills**
The text emphasizes active listening and pronunciation practice. Students are tasked with analyzing the language of videos, specifically regarding world Englishes and subtitle usage in documentaries. The "Read It Out" exercises guide students through observing verb patterns, such as -ed endings and -s plurals, to improve their speaking clarity and pronunciation.

**Group Collaboration and Critical Thinking**
The final section challenges students to act as members of the International Olympic Committee. Students must evaluate potential new Olympic sports based on four critical criteria:
1. Challenge level (physical and mental).
2. Entertainment value.
3. Universal accessibility.
4. Inclusivity for people with disabilities.

Students use these benchmarks to brainstorm, debate, and propose a new sport, requiring them to consider potential adaptations that would make athletic competition more equitable for all participants.

This section focuses on the development of empathy and its role in human connection. Here is a summary of the key topics covered in pages [S.47] through [S.52]:

Core Concepts of Empathy
The curriculum explores the fundamental definition of empathy, specifically how it differs from sympathy, methods to cultivate empathy in daily life, and how empathy influences cross-cultural communication [S.47].

Educational Resources
Students are provided with instructional lectures and presentations that include:
- A main presentation by Annie regarding the core definition and application of empathy [S.49].
- An extra presentation highlighting the practical benefits of how empathy can assist us in our interactions [S.50].

Practical Skills Modules
The textbook provides a series of three short videos designed to enhance emotional and social intelligence:
1. Active Listening: Techniques for better communication [S.51].
2. Phone Anxiety: Strategies for overcoming communication barriers [S.51].
3. Coping with Stress: Managing emotional well-being [S.51].

This document outlines a lesson module regarding superstitions, focusing on their origins and influence. The content covers the following key areas:

Main Presentation: The core material introduces the concept of superstitions, prompting students to explore their definitions and historical origins. The lesson identifies four specific sources or origins for superstitions and examines the historical reasoning behind cultural practices, such as "knocking on wood" (p. 55).

Practical Examples and Discussion: The text provides specific scenarios, such as the superstition regarding bananas on fishing boats, to illustrate how these beliefs manifest in real-world contexts. It also encourages students to analyze the role of coincidence in forming superstitious beliefs and to reflect on their own experiences with luck (p. 55).

Digital Integration: The material includes links to interactive digital content, such as videos and extra presentations, to facilitate remote or mobile learning. This includes a dedicated session on the prevalence of superstitions within professional work environments (p. 56, 57).

This section provides an educational overview of language, memory, and aphasia, focusing on stroke awareness and speech patterns (p. 59-66).

Key topics include:

Aphasia Overview
Aphasia is a condition affecting communication abilities. It can be caused by various factors, including strokes, brain tumors, injuries, trauma, or specific diseases (p. 61, 64). A primary characteristic of aphasia involves word substitutions, known as paraphasias, where individuals might unintentionally replace target words with incorrect ones (p. 64).

Stroke Awareness and Emergency Response
The text emphasizes the importance of recognizing the signs of a stroke using the FAST acronym (p. 63). This mnemonic helps bystanders identify symptoms quickly, which is critical for potentially life-saving intervention (p. 63).

Linguistic Practice: Content vs. Function Words
A significant portion of the material focuses on English speech patterns by distinguishing between content words and function words (p. 64). 
- Content words carry the primary meaning and are typically stressed or emphasized. 
- Function words are reduced in speech and carry less weight. 
Learners are encouraged to practice reading sentences aloud by stressing content words to improve communication clarity (p. 64, 65).

This section of the text focuses on nutrition education, provided through lectures, vocabulary studies, and a conversational dialog. The key points are structured below:

**Nutrition Lecture Details**
The presentation covers the fundamental components of human nutrition:
* Macronutrients (protein, carbohydrates, and fat) are primarily responsible for providing energy to the body (p. 69).
* Micronutrients (vitamins and minerals) are required by the body in small doses (p. 70).
* A balanced diet is achieved by consuming a variety of macro- and micronutrients, often visualized by eating a diverse spectrum of food colors (p. 71).
* Dietary recommendations for those who exercise regularly emphasize a high intake of carbohydrates, specifically around 60% of total intake (p. 70).

**Vocabulary and Language Development**
The text provides resources to support language acquisition related to health, including:
* A comprehensive vocabulary list featuring terms such as "essential," "moderation," "promote," and "recommendation" (p. 73-74).
* A specific focus on pronunciation, targeting the short "e" vowel sound (p. 77).

**Practical Application: Health Dialog**
The final portion of the section examines a practical scenario involving family health:
* A conversation is presented regarding an elderly father’s medical visit, covering dietary adjustments, doctor-recommended lifestyle changes, and the fulfillment of prescriptions (p. 75-76).
* Listeners are encouraged to identify specific health requirements and family dynamics through these recorded interactions (p. 76).

This section contains a collection of listening comprehension modules designed for ESOL (English for Speakers of Other Languages) learners, focusing on news-style audio content and interactive language practice. The materials cover pages [S.79] through [S.88] and are structured as follows:

**1. Content Overview**
The section provides four distinct news-based listening topics:
*   Minh Pham: A profile of local flower shop interactions ([S.81]-[S.82]).
*   Choi Kimchi: A story tracking the business growth from farmer's markets to supermarkets ([S.83]-[S.84]).
*   Antarctica Expedition: A report on a Portland man who became the first person to walk across Antarctica alone ([S.85]-[S.86]).
*   Baseball History: A historical overview of baseball in the United States, with a specific focus on Portland, Oregon ([S.87]-[S.88]).

**2. Learning Methodology**
Each topic utilizes a standardized pedagogical approach:
*   Audio Integration: Learners listen to news articles via embedded links to external media platforms ([S.81], [S.83], [S.85], [S.87]).
*   Interactive Assessments: Learners complete H5P exercises and comprehension questions linked online to verify their understanding ([S.81], [S.84], [S.85], [S.87]).
*   Critical Thinking: Some sections, such as the feature on Minh Pham, include additional discussion prompts to encourage learners to express opinions and analyze character traits ([S.82]).
*   Supplementary Resources: For complex stories, such as the Antarctica expedition, additional video links are provided to reinforce the auditory content through visual media ([S.86]).

This section covers diverse topics ranging from news-based listening exercises to in-depth English pronunciation guides. The content can be summarized into two main components:

**News and Comprehension Practice (Pages [S.89]–[S.98])**
The text provides several news-related segments designed for English language learners, focusing on topics such as:
- Animal behavior (cats and their humans).
- Oregon-specific news, including election systems, local lawsuits, lottery winners, environmental issues (recycling), and unique food innovations (chickpea-based ice cream).
- Each topic includes listening opportunities and interactive exercises to build comprehension, vocabulary, and reporting skills like identifying the 5 Ws.

**Pronunciation and Grammar (Pages [S.99]–[S.110])**
The second half of the document serves as a guide for mastering American English pronunciation and irregular verb forms:
- Vowel Sounds and IPA: It introduces the International Phonetic Alphabet (IPA) as a tool for clear pronunciation, mapping 12 distinct American vowel sounds to common color words.
- Irregular Past Tense Verbs: The text provides a list of common irregular verbs, categorized by their vowel sounds to assist with correct conjugation and pronunciation.
- Consonants: The guide explains the physiological difference between voiced and voiceless sounds. It demonstrates how vocal cords vibrate during voiced sounds (e.g., /b/, /d/, /g/) versus remaining still during voiceless sounds (e.g., /p/, /t/, /k/). It includes phonetic charts to help learners practice these physical sound distinctions.

This section covers essential pronunciation features of American English, focusing on consonant endings, specific letter sounds, and word stress patterns.

Final S and ED Sounds
The pronunciation of final suffixes depends on the sound immediately preceding them:
- Final S: Voiceless consonants result in /s/, voiced consonants result in /z/, and words ending in s, z, sh, zh, ch, j, or x result in /ɪz/ (p. 113-114).
- Final ED: Voiceless consonants result in /t/, voiced consonants result in /d/, and words ending in /t/ or /d/ sounds result in /ɪd/ (p. 117-118).

T Pronunciation
American English T sounds are categorized into four types:
- Normal T: Found at the beginning of words or after most consonants (except R) (p. 112).
- Flap T: A quick /d/ sound occurring when a T is between two vowels (e.g., "water"), unless the T falls in a stressed syllable (p. 113-114).
- Glottal Stop: A stoppage of air often found in T+N combinations or specific contractions like "can't" (p. 114-115).
- Other variations: T can shift to a /tʃ/ sound (e.g., "picture") or an /ʃ/ sound (e.g., "station") depending on surrounding letters (p. 115-116).

Word Stress
Stress marks a syllable as longer, louder, higher in pitch, and clearer (p. 120).
- Compound Nouns: Typically stress the first word or component (e.g., "classmate") (p. 121).
- Adjective/Noun Combinations: Stress generally falls on the noun, as it carries more weight than the adjective (p. 121-122).
- Numbers: Almost always receive stress in standard speech (p. 122).

This section focuses on the mechanics of spoken American English, specifically how stress and reduction contribute to fluency and comprehension.

Key Concepts of Stress and Reduction
Stress involves making a syllable louder, longer, higher, and clearer (p. 134). By contrast, reduction creates a contrast to stress: syllables become quicker, quieter, smaller, and less clear, often shifting vowels to a schwa sound /ə/ (p. 135). Mastering these reductions makes speech sound more natural and helps listeners identify main ideas (p. 136).

Content vs. Function Words
Sentences generally prioritize stress on content words—nouns, verbs, adjectives, and adverbs—while function words like prepositions, articles, and conjunctions remain unstressed (p. 138). Understanding this distinction helps learners identify the primary message of a sentence even when speed increases (p. 138).

Common Reduction Patterns
Specific examples provided in the text include:
1. The word "and" is frequently reduced to /ən/ in common idioms like "peace and quiet" or "bread and butter" (p. 137).
2. Pronouns such as "her," "him," "his," and "hers" often lose the "h" sound when they appear in the middle of a sentence, such as changing "I like him" to "I likim" (p. 141). However, when these pronouns start a sentence, they retain the full "h" sound and stress (p. 141).

Additional Resources
The text concludes by noting that these materials are part of an open educational resource (OER) designed for remote teaching. It offers teachers various tools, including lectures, vocabulary activities, and multimedia pronunciation exercises, all of which are available for adaptation under a Creative Commons license (p. 143, 144).
Here is a quiz for this lesson.

questions.question: According to the unit on introverts and extroverts, what is the 'gray area' mentioned in the text?
options: The tendency for some people to be shy while others are outgoing.
The specific environment where introverts feel most energized.
The fact that personality traits are not strictly binary (black and white).
The confusion people feel when figuring out their personality type.
questions.explanation: The text states that introversion and extroversion are not completely 'black and white' and that there is a 'gray area,' suggesting people don't necessarily have to be exclusively one or the other.

questions.question: In the context of the lecture on Parasports, why is 'adapted equipment' significant?
options: It allows athletes with different physical impairments to participate and compete.
It makes the sports more expensive for the Olympic Committee.
It is used only by athletes who compete in the winter games.
It proves that able-bodied athletes are better at adapting to new challenges.
questions.explanation: Adapted equipment allows for the inclusion of athletes with various impairments, enabling them to compete in sports through modifications designed for their specific needs.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the core concept used in the 'I AM' activity?
options: Reflecting on one's own motivations, challenges, and personal values.
Describing the rules of a new sport you want to add to the Olympics.
Listing your physical characteristics and favorite sports.
Reporting on the professional achievements of famous Olympic athletes.
questions.explanation: The 'I AM' activity asks students to reflect on their own lives, motivations, and the challenges they have overcome to achieve their goals, rather than focusing purely on external facts.

questions.question: During the group activity to create a 'New Olympic Sport,' why is it important to ask if the activity is 'open to everyone'?
options: To determine if the sport can be marketed as a professional job.
To verify if the sport is already popular in Oregon.
To ensure that spectators can watch it on television without paying.
To consider accessibility across different groups, including various ages and physical abilities.
questions.explanation: The inclusivity question evaluates whether the sport can be played by people across different backgrounds, ages, and physical abilities, which is a key factor in Olympic eligibility.

questions.question: What is the primary goal of the 'Read it out' exercises regarding pronunciation?
options: To ensure students can read aloud without ever making a mistake.
To memorize the exact definition of every word in the article.
To improve clarity and comfort with specific sound patterns, such as '-ed' endings or consonant clusters.
To increase the speed at which the student can speak English.
questions.explanation: The exercises focus on individual pronunciation challenges—like '-ed' endings, '-s' plurals, and difficult consonant sounds—to help students speak more clearly and accurately.

cards.front: How do introverts typically gain energy?
cards.back: Introverts gain energy from their internal world, such as deep thinking, contemplating new ideas, and reflecting on experiences.
An ambivert is someone who falls in the 'gray area' between being an introvert and an extrovert, rather than being strictly one or the other.

cards.front: What is a major misconception about introversion and extroversion?
cards.back: The idea that you must be one or the other; in reality, it is not a black-and-white scale.

cards.front: What is the primary function of the Paralympic Games?
cards.back: They are a major international sports event for athletes with various physical disabilities, including amputations and blindness.

cards.front: What year did the Paralympic Winter Games open?
What are three key considerations when evaluating a potential new Olympic sport?
cards.back: Is it challenging, is it entertaining to watch, and is it open/accessible to everyone?

cards.front: What is the English idiom 'hang out with'?
cards.back: It means to spend time socially with someone.

cards.front: How can you differentiate between sympathy and empathy?
cards.back: Sympathy is feeling for someone, whereas empathy involves understanding and sharing the feelings of another person.

cards.front: What does it mean to 'deal with' something?
cards.back: It refers to managing, handling, or addressing a specific situation or topic.

cards.front: What is the general objective of the 'I AM' activity regarding personal growth?
cards.back: To identify and describe your own accomplishments, passions, and challenges to better understand what motivates you.

slides.title: Intermediate English Communication
bullets: Focusing on high-intermediate communication skills.
Exploring personality, diversity, and language nuances.
slides.notes: Welcome to our intermediate English course. We will focus on improving communication through engaging topics and structured practice.

slides.title: Introverts and Extroverts
bullets: Understand personality types and social energy.
Define key terms: introverts, extroverts, ambiverts.
Learn vocabulary like 'misconception' and 'unique' (p. 5).
Identify your own personality type through reflection.
slides.notes: We begin by analyzing how different personalities gain or lose energy in social settings.

slides.title: Sports, Disabilities, and Aging
bullets: Explore the history of the Paralympic Games (p. 27).
Discuss athletes overcoming challenges and adversity.
Practice active listening through sports-focused media.
Define terms like 'able-bodied' and 'to pursue a dream' (p. 26).
slides.notes: This chapter examines how individuals overcome physical and personal hurdles to achieve excellence.

slides.title: Understanding Empathy
bullets: Distinguish empathy from sympathy.
Analyze the role of empathy in cross-cultural communication.
Discuss strategies to improve empathetic listening.
Examine links between stress and emotional regulation.
slides.notes: Empathy is a foundational skill for effective long-term communication and cultural understanding.

slides.title: Superstitions and Culture
bullets: Examine the four main origins of superstitions.
Discuss how superstitions impact daily life and work.
Explore the role of coincidence in belief systems.
Analyze cultural anecdotes like 'knocking on wood'.
slides.notes: We look at why humans create superstitions and how they serve as cultural markers.

slides.title: Language, Memory, and Aphasia
bullets: Understand aphasia causes and main types (p. 61).
Recognize the F.A.S.T. signs of a stroke (p. 53).
Practice stressing content words versus function words (p. 64).
Learn strategies for communicating with aphasia patients.
slides.notes: This session bridges medical awareness with practical communication and pronunciation instruction.

slides.title: Food and Nutrition
bullets: Analyze macronutrients and micronutrients (p. 69).
Discuss healthy, balanced dietary habits.
Practice listening skills via health-related conversations (p. 75).
Master vocabulary related to nutrition and health.
slides.notes: We apply technical vocabulary to describe nutrition while honing our conversational listening skills.

slides.title: Advanced Pronunciation
bullets: Master voiced and voiceless consonant differences (p. 101).
Learn rules for final 's' and '-ed' endings (p. 103, 107).
Practice 'Flap T' patterns in American English (p. 113).
Understand word stress and reduced syllables (p. 124).
slides.notes: Pronunciation is key to clarity; we focus on phonetic habits that make speech sound more natural.

slides.title: Content, Function, and Reductions
bullets: Identify stressed content words to improve comprehension.
Practice reducing function words like 'and', 'him', and 'her'.
Use reduction to improve fluency in spoken English (p. 126).
Target specific pronunciation challenges for better clarity.
slides.notes: Reducing function words and emphasizing content is the secret to sounding like a natural English speaker.
